cases.title: Collapse on the Kitchen Floor
cases.presentation: A 70-year-old woman collapses at home. She is unresponsive, not breathing normally, and pulseless. You are alone, have called 911 on speaker, and are trained in CPR. She is on a tile kitchen floor.
cases.question: How should you perform compressions?
options: A: Rate 60/min, depth 1 inch, hands on the upper sternum.
B: Rate 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 inches on the lower half of the sternum, full recoil, 30:2 with breaths.
C: Continuous fast shallow compressions at 140/min.
D: Give breaths first for 2 minutes, then start compressions.
cases.explanation: High-quality adult CPR is 100–120/min at 2–2.4 inches on the lower half of the sternum with full recoil, in a 30:2 ratio for a single trained rescuer. The firm floor is ideal for effective depth.
cases.clinicalPearl: Push to a 100–120 bpm tempo and let the chest fully rebound each time — depth and recoil matter as much as rate.

cases.title: Untrained Bystander
cases.presentation: A man collapses on a train platform. A bystander who has never taken a CPR class is on the phone with the dispatcher. The man is unresponsive with agonal gasps and no pulse. The bystander is anxious about mouth-to-mouth.
cases.question: What should the dispatcher coach the bystander to do?
options: A: Wait for EMS without touching the patient.
B: Perform compression-only (hands-only) CPR: push hard and fast in the center of the chest continuously.
cases.explanation: For an adult in sudden collapse with an untrained or unwilling rescuer, compression-only CPR is recommended. Continuous hard, fast compressions maintain circulation until help arrives, and any CPR beats none.
cases.clinicalPearl: Removing the mouth-to-mouth barrier gets more bystanders to act — hands-only CPR saves lives.

cases.title: Fatigue and Falling Depth
cases.presentation: You have been doing single-rescuer CPR for nearly 3 minutes. A feedback device shows your compressions have drifted to about 1.5 inches deep and your rate has crept up. A second trained responder has just arrived with an AED.
cases.question: What are the two best immediate actions?
options: A: Keep compressing alone and ignore the device.
B: Quickly switch compressors (<10 s) to restore depth and apply the AED as soon as possible.
C: Stop CPR to rest for a minute.
D: Slow down to 60/min to regain depth.
cases.explanation: Fatigue degrades depth within a couple of minutes. Swapping compressors quickly restores high-quality compressions, and applying the AED enables early defibrillation — both improve the chance of survival while keeping interruptions minimal.
cases.clinicalPearl: Feedback devices catch the silent decay of depth and rate that rescuers rarely notice themselves.
